TextENGNew: "Whoa, now — you really pulled out all the stops for this seat. ...Is this all right? I haven't been to a restaurant that brings lacquered chopsticks instead of disposables in a long time."
"Ha ha ha, what are you saying, Chief Prosecutor, sir. Here — let's begin with a drink... Sorimachi-san."
As I said that, I reached for the bottle of beer the attendant had just brought in... and offered to pour for the man sitting across from me — Chief Prosecutor Sorimachi.
He was an upperclassman of mine from our university days... and now a prosecuting attorney attached to the Special Investigation Department of the Tokyo District Prosecutors Office.

A sharp prosecutor who had taken on major cases from the day of his appointment — yet for all his competence, opinion among his male peers was sharply divided, thanks to a handsome face that drew women and a manner of speaking that was rough around the edges.
changeReason: Old says 'womanizing demeanor' implying he's a womanizer; new says 'handsome face that drew women,' which matches the Japanese 女好きのする端正な容姿 (refined looks that appeal to women) without implying he's a womanizer.

TextENGNew: Even so, he had looked out for me since our seminar days, and by now we were close enough that our families spent time together.
changeReason: Old says 'felt as close as family'; new says 'close enough that our families spent time together.' The Japanese 家族ぐるみで付き合う specifically means socializing as families, which the new captures more precisely.

TextENGNew: "Oh — thanks. ...Mmm, that's ice cold."
He caught the golden liquid that had been poured to the brim of his glass, then took the bottle from my hands and returned the courtesy.
I accepted gratefully, and we raised our glasses... and touched them together with a soft clink.

"...Ahh. I tell you, beer tastes something special when you drink it in a place like this. And that feeling of it seeping through every part of you — haven't felt that in a while, and it's quite something."
"A while...? That doesn't sound like the man I knew — the legendary heavy drinker. Have you been drinking less lately?"
"More or less... I stop in at a little standing bar on the way home, just enough to take the edge off."

"It's partly the busy schedule... but if I go deep into the bottle with the prosecutors crowd, I don't get home until the small hours. And if that's the case, I'd rather be home early to spend time with Yuuichi."
"...I see."
As I refilled Sorimachi-san's empty glass, I thought inwardly: what a remarkable man.
His wife had died not long after they married. It seemed she had broken down in health after giving birth to their son.
Since then, Sorimachi-san had raised his eldest son Yuuichi-kun on his own, with his parents helping where they could.

As chief of the Special Investigation Department, he was pressed into exhausting work day after day... one could easily imagine what that kind of endurance must cost him, yet he never let a trace of it show.

It meant that some superiors and peers looked down on him for being poor company in the evenings — but he devoted himself to his family without a glance at other women... Beneath that appearance, he was a genuinely sincere person.
"Measured against you, Sorimachi-san, I still have a very long way to go."

"There was a drama recital at my daughter's kindergarten a little while back... I had to miss it because of work, and she sulked terribly at me for it. It took quite a lot of effort to win her back into a good mood."
"Hahaha, that was bad luck. Still — this is the age when Miyuki-chan most wants her father close. If that keeps up, you'll be in real trouble once she hits adolescence."
"Yes, I'm taking that to heart. And yet — balancing work and home is what makes you a proper adult... I hear that from my wife often enough, but it isn't as simple as the words make it sound."
"Watching you manage it with such care — I genuinely think you're remarkable."
"Well, in that respect the feeling is mutual. I've lost count of how many times I've let something slip or forgotten something entirely, only to kick myself over it later."
"...But even so, I refuse to use busy as an excuse for it. That was the promise I made to my wife."
"...Sorimachi-san."
His late wife had been his childhood sweetheart, and by all accounts he was a devoted husband in a way that was the talk of the office.
He never shed a tear in front of others — but at the funeral, I happened to glimpse him at the edge of the grounds, weeping with his voice pressed down to nothing.

I stood there unable to move, overwhelmed and unsure what to say — and he looked back at me, his eyes scarlet, with a bitter smile that seemed to say, '...Keep this between us.' I have never been able to forget that face.

"....
So then, remarriage is probably off the table for the foreseeable future. What a waste — truly."
"Whenever we used to go drinking at the clubs together, you'd end up monopolizing all the beautiful women there before the night was out — my boss used to complain about it bitterly."
"Did you know? As long as you were willing, any number of candidates would step forward, from what I understand."
changeReason: Old translates 知っていますか as 'You knew, didn't you?' (assuming knowledge); new correctly translates as 'Did you know?' (genuinely asking).

TextENGNew: "Ha ha ha, as if! Those kinds of women bolt the moment they find out I come with a child in tow."
"That's the sort who come chasing money and a title. I'm afraid it's a collective no thank you to every last one of that crowd."
"Hahaha — I knew you'd say that."
...Not that I imagined it was universally so. There were surely women among them who genuinely liked Sorimachi-san for the man he was, and drew close for that reason.
But the firm wall he raised even toward those women — I believed that was his own way of keeping faith with his late wife.
"Well... a woman who could truly surpass her isn't the sort you find very easily."

"She was so kind, so beautiful... when you introduced her to us in the university seminar for the first time, every one of us let out a sigh — thinking, so this is what people mean when they say Madonna."
"There were those who said it had to be trickery on your part, or that you had some hold over her."
"Come now, that's putting her on too high a pedestal."
"She looked the part, I'll grant you that... but she had quite a fierce side too. When none of you were watching, she'd give me a proper thrashing, you know."
"The day she found the matches from the cabaret bar — I genuinely thought I was going to be killed..."
"Is... that so? That is very surprising..."

I had only spoken with Sorimachi-san's wife once or twice before her death, but she was someone to whom the words ideal wife applied with perfect precision. I had never seen an angry expression on her face.
Though surely that was the reverse side of their closeness — and whatever jealousy she showed must have been grounded in real depth of feeling.
Well... measured against a woman like that, it was only natural that even a crowd of reasonably beautiful women would seem to pale into nothing.
"Come — it's not as though I'm keeping some vow of fidelity."

"Thinking of Yuuichi, I know there are ways in which having a mother versus not having one affects a child's emotional development. If the opportunity arose, well — I wouldn't say I wouldn't consider it."
"Oh, really? And — if I may ask — does your preference still run to the quieter, more refined type, as she was, rather than the loud and headstrong kind?"
"Stop with the refined. If pressed... I'd say a woman with beautiful eyes, and convictions of her own."
"But not so assertive that she drowns out the people around her... a certain modesty, that's the ideal. If I ever meet someone like that, I'll give up the widower's life on the spot. Ahahahah!"
"Th-those are demanding conditions... oh —"
Just then, with perfect timing, the sliding door to the private room opened and an attendant brought in lacquered trays bearing the courses.
changeReason: Old says 'waitress' with 'plates on our table'; new says 'attendant' with 'lacquered trays bearing the courses,' which more accurately reflects 仲居さん (traditional Japanese restaurant attendant) and 足つきの膳 (legged trays).

TextENGNew: She set them before Sorimachi-san and me with careful, unhurried movements, explained each dish in turn, then departed with quiet steps.
"Still... why is it that high-end restaurants feel the need to explain the dishes in advance? You learn everything before you've taken a bite — where's the pleasure in that?"

"Hahaha... there is that. And yet, wouldn't something feel lacking if you weren't at least told that the fish is yamame trout, or sweetfish, or what have you? We're not gourmands — we can't tell the difference by taste alone."
"Hmm. There is that way of thinking too."
Saying so, Sorimachi-san deftly separated the flesh of his grilled fish from the bone. Seeing that, I too reached for my chopsticks.
"...Oh, speaking of fish — Yuuichi said again that he'd like you to take him fishing sometime."
"Yuuichi-kun said that...?"
"He seems to have taken quite a liking to it. Apparently he went to a river nearby recently with a few friends. ...Though all they caught were muddy crucian carp, so there was nothing fit to eat."
"Hahaha, that's wonderful. Do warn him to be careful near the water, though. He's still in primary school — a fall into the river would be no small matter."
"You're right, I'll have a word with him next time. ...Oh, and he'd like to see Miyuki-chan again."
"...Oh? Miyuki and..."
My chopsticks, which had been working at the bones of my fish, stopped dead.
Come to think of it, the last time we all went stream fishing together... those two had been chatting away like old friends.
Miyuki wasn't shy around strangers, so I hadn't thought it odd... but still...
"...Hey, what are you stiffening up for? Don't tell me you're going to start playing the overprotective father already."
"Huh? Ah — ahaha... of course not, nothing like that. They're still a primary schooler and a kindergartener, after all. Something like that is a pleasure for a very much later stage of life..."
"...Well — in my case, I first met my wife on a kindergarten field trip."
"Bwah?!"
The beer I had hastily drunk to calm myself went down the wrong way, and I choked helplessly.
Sorimachi-san watched my fluster with an amused grin... then abruptly picked up an unopened beer bottle, drew out the stopper, and murmured, almost to himself.
"...So then?"
"Pardon?"
"Let's hear it now. You arranged this whole dinner for a reason, didn't you? ...As I said — I can't afford to linger over drinks until all hours. Get to the point."
At those words, I shifted my legs and straightened my posture, drew a slow breath, and brought my thoughts to order.
...I knew it was cowardly, leaning on the courage that alcohol provides.
But without it... I wouldn't have had the nerve to say this.

"...
Public Security has decided... to withdraw from the investigation. ...I'm sorry."
"I see..."
More calmly than I had expected, Sorimachi-san poured beer into both our glasses. He raised his and drank it down in one long swallow, then shrugged as though resigned, and let out a breath.
"Well — I'd had a feeling something like this might happen for a while now. This isn't your fault... so don't you apologize for it."
"Sorimachi-san..."
"You're treating me like a stranger, and I don't care for it. I'm not one for easy familiarity — but come now, it's you and me."
"If you had to go out of your way to smooth my feathers every time something like this came up, your wallet would never be full enough. ...That quality of yours — I don't dislike it."

Having been told not to apologize, to apologize further would itself have been rude — so I bowed my head in silence. ...Even so, I could not help the weight of guilt and remorse that would not leave me.
...The matter we had been cooperating on was the misconduct of a certain politician.
They had been routing confidential domestic intelligence through a certain foreign-affiliated company... and accepting bribes in return — that was the core of what we were working to establish.

Furthermore, recent investigations had brought to light the figure lurking behind all of this bribery activity — a certain political merchant who had wielded dominant influence over cabinet ministers since the postwar era, and who had died some months earlier.
The scale of the organization he had built was comparable to the Lowell Scandal — the pharmaceutical scandal that had once erupted as a major incident.

To that end, Sorimachi-san and the rest of the Tokyo District Prosecutors Office Special Investigation Department had been throwing every available resource into a thorough investigation of its full scope and details.
And for them, the intelligence-gathering and diversionary activities of our Public Security Bureau were indispensable.

Moreover, in a case where the targets were likely politicians or politically connected businessmen, it was extremely difficult for the police alone to secure grounds for arrest — making cooperation with the Special Investigation Department the most direct route.
That was precisely why two parties who would not ordinarily collaborate had joined forces for this investigation... and yet.
changeReason: Old ends with an ellipsis without capturing the contrastive が in the Japanese; new adds 'and yet,' conveying the unfinished but implied contrast.

TextENGNew: "To be honest... it's a bitter disappointment. We had only just managed to obtain documents from the other side through a whistleblower..."

"That's how it goes. ...Or rather — the fact that they moved on you so aggressively is proof enough of how damaging what you obtained truly is. They wouldn't have resorted to such heavy-handed interference otherwise."
"Sorimachi-san..."

"...
Incidentally — those documents you obtained, there's no possibility of sharing them... is there?"
"...I'm sorry."
"Hahaha — I know, I know. Public Security exists to protect the national interest — to maintain the stability of the state. That's the whole point."
changeReason: Old translates わかってるって as 'I thought so'; new translates as 'I know, I know,' which better matches the casual reassurance in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: "...Don't take that as sarcasm, by the way. That tension is, in the end, the reason why the Prosecutors Office and Public Security have never been able to fully see eye to eye."
As he spoke, Sorimachi-san turned his empty glass in his hands and gazed into whatever he found in its depths.
Seeing that, I reached for the beer bottle beside me to offer him more — but he raised one hand to stop me, then slowly opened his mouth to speak.
"That political merchant... Koizumi — he was a thoroughly rotten piece of work."

"He parcelled out the national budget and political party donations at his own whim, gathered people into his private research groups and study circles, scattered money among his proteges... and quietly built his power."
"He knew full well that this is a nation governed by law, a parliamentary democracy — and he openly made a mockery of it. ...That is the one thing I cannot forgive."
"...Yes. Not at all."
"And yet he died not long ago. A full natural life, amid every mark of success and warm remembrance... and his honours intact."

"...An ending that would offend any god or Buddha, and I resent it — but setting that aside, the organization still stands. And men sharper and more ruthless than Koizumi are running it now, lining their pockets as they go."
"Bringing that to ruin is the duty of our Special Investigation Department. Not as speakers of justice — as custodians of the rule of law."

"...But Akasaka — you and yours are different. Getting swept up in feeling and losing sight of your position and your duties — we're not asking for that either. ...Do keep watching over things for us, won't you."
"Yes. ...As a younger colleague — I'm rooting for you."
I gave that answer and extended my hand.
For a moment Sorimachi-san's eyes went wide... but before long, looking faintly embarrassed, he took it and gripped it firmly.
...Perhaps it was a naive gesture. Perhaps he'd think it ridiculous.
But at that moment... it was truly the only thing I could do.
"...A trip, you said?"
"Yes. There have been so many days lately when I couldn't even make it home, let alone take a rest — I thought I'd ask for a longer stretch of leave and take us somewhere with a hot spring..."
...Some days after the dinner with Sorimachi-san.
Having been given the time off, I took my wife and daughter and set out for a department store.

I suppose it was because I hadn't managed to give them any proper family time for a while — when I'd suggested it, both of them looked at me with some suspicion — but the weather was kind, and we had a good time.

Miyuki in particular seemed overjoyed to have a chance to play to her heart's content on the rooftop plaza... and after a somewhat late lunch at the restaurant, she was in beaming high spirits the rest of the afternoon.

Her good mood was such that she ended up striking up conversation with a perfect stranger at the next table, and Yukie, unable to watch any further, had to intervene with a firm: "Don't talk to people you don't know."
"Well — what do you say, Yukie? You've always liked hot springs, haven't you?"
"Yes, well... I suppose. But are you sure it's all right? You've hardly ever taken such a long holiday before..."
"Oh, don't worry. The section chief himself told me to go and rest for a bit. ...Miyuki, what about you? Would you like to come to a hot spring?"
"Hot spring...? Is that like a bath house?"
"Ahaha, no no. A hot spring is a large outdoor bath in the mountains. We'd stay at a hotel, eat wonderful food. You could even do some fishing — I'm sure there'd be somewhere for that."
"Oh...! So we're going on a trip?! Yaaay, hot spring, hot spring! I'm so happy!"
Miyuki threw herself into excitement so vigorously she nearly knocked her juice glass off the table.
In her exuberance in the small space, a fork slid from the table, and its sharp high clatter rang out through the restaurant.
"Miyuki, be quiet! We're not at home — you'll bother everyone around you if you're noisy like this."
"Look — the fork fell. I'll have to ask them to replace it... Excuse me!"
"...I'm sorry."
Sharply scolded by Yukie, Miyuki shrank back with tears in her eyes.

But it was my bringing up the hot spring that had gotten her excited in the first place. With that in mind, I gently intervened with a "now, now," and forestalled Yukie before she could continue the lecture.
"Yukie... don't be so hard on her. She's at the age where she wants to make noise — can't you overlook it just this once?"
"No. This kind of discipline needs to be instilled firmly while she's still young, or it won't do her any good. The teacher at the kindergarten has already mentioned she has trouble sitting still."
"Even so..."
"And besides, other children have it so much harder. Lessons, extra activities... Miyuki is actually quite well off by comparison."
"You don't know what it's like because you're never here — that's why you can say such easy things."
"...I'm sorry."
"No — I'm sorry! I got carried away with what I was saying... I know very well what your work demands, I really do...!"
With genuine contrition on her face, Yukie turned to me and bowed her head again and again.
Gentle by nature, and never once having complained about her husband's work — she was far more than I deserved.
The fact that she had slipped into a sharper tone without meaning to was probably because she was carrying tiredness and worries she couldn't voice to me.
...That thought only deepened the guilt I already felt.
"No... You're right about Miyuki. I've been leaving everything at home entirely to you for a while now, and you've had to shoulder so much of the difficulty. I'm truly sorry for that."
"My love..."
"So this time — let the trip be a rest for you, won't you? I'll take good care of Miyuki."
"You can't mean that — you're exhausted yourself. You're the one who needs to rest."
"No, it's you who —"
"No, you are the one —"
At that moment, Miyuki, who had been watching us pass the kindness back and forth with wide blank eyes, suddenly broke into a bright smile.
"...Ahaha! Daddy and Mummy are being all lovey-dovey!"
"What?!"
L-lovey-dovey...?
The unexpected words left us staring at each other. And in an instant, a wave of embarrassment rose in both of us simultaneously — we both looked away, flushing scarlet to the neck.
"Ooh, Daddy and Mummy have gone all red! So warm, you two! Woo-hoo!"
"Mi-Miyuki!"
"Hey — where did you learn a word like that?!"
"Kindergarten! Morita-sensei taught us!"
The formidable power of modern kindergarten education. ...I think I need to have a quiet conversation with that teacher at some point.
For the time being I cleared my throat several times to cover my embarrassment and composed myself... then turned back to Yukie and returned to the matter at hand.
"A-anyway — we can take turns looking after Miyuki, and we'll both relax and unwind at the hot spring. What do you say?"
"Yes... well, if that would suit you..."
"Good. In that case — I'll go for a look-ahead visit this coming Sunday. For the destination... what about Wakura Onsen?"
